speaker: interviewer
text: --with Alberta--

speaker: interviewee
text: I see we are continuing--

speaker: interviewer
text: Szalita.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I did-- I didn't understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's not finished! You misunderstood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a second-- just a second. Today is December 11, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. I didn't understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Now you-- I didn't understand. I don't know how it-- so I am in Paris. In Paris, I came to Paris with a-- a friend of mine, Shimona. She's now-- she's in-- she is in Paris with her and Michael, that man-- that fellow who adopted me that I didn't mention yet. I told you about it. And I came with the idea that I will be three months at the-- at the Institute of Research in-- in Paris, in neurology. When I arrived to-- to Paris, I knew in-- in Warsaw, I did-- I was a general secretary for the organization tours, which is OZE. [NON-ENGLISH] was the [NON-ENGLISH]. It was such a name-- Health Service for-- Health Services for Jewish Population.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- some of those Jewish doctors that we got together-- revived that organization to take care of people who came from Auschwitz and from other places. And they needed health care. And the Polish government was not yet ready to be able to provide this health services. So we organized ourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as a result of this organization, in 19-- in 1945, I went with five other doctors. We went to Switzerland to get help from the American Joint Distribution Committee to be sent to Poland and to make them-- to notify them what is what there, and what is needed. And I met people from the American Joint Distribution Committee like Schmidt-- William Schmidt, and other people, and also with their union. These are Russian-- it was a Russian organization. And some of them lived in Switzerland. And they worked in Paris. So I got to know those people. And we got the help that was supposed to come.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I came to Paris, I got in touch with these other union people. I got in touch with them, so they offered me a job right there in Paris-- a medical administrator for health services for the surviving Jews. So I had to discuss it with that-- I mean-- [NON-ENGLISH] whether it's all right with him that I don't take the fellowship to the offer to this whatever-- and take the job, because we needed more-- we needed more money for to-- to-- to support ourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got the job, a-- a Director of this health services, the medical director of their union. They needed-- they really needed me. That's it. So I didn't do it-- I didn't do it for-- to just to provide me with a job. Because they needed-- they needed to replace the one they had. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's how I started the work in Paris as a medical director for their union. And that was very much in touch with-- by-- with American Joint Distribution Committee. What did you see, and what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see? What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I do?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, it was necessary-- first of all, I was-- I went to-- to the DP camps in Germany where I was supposed to settle a strike to the Jewish physicians who were themself inmates of the DP camps. They were striking. They didn't want to work as doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah. I settled that strike with them in this kind of a way, that each one of them will get a carton of cigarettes a week. Plus, they wanted books about-- new-- new books that came out about medicine during the war. And I went from Germany, to Switzerland, to Geneva to got-- to get them their books. And their cigarettes, they got from that-- from-- from the American government. It was a combination of work of UNRRA, OZE, and American Joint Distribution Committee that I was functioning there in Germany. And this-- in that-- in the DP camps, they were fed, naturally. And they were playing cards. We didn't know what much what to do. In Switzerland, they thought that there is no children, and no more Jewish children. And then, most of the women there were pregnant.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember that I was telling them in Switzerland, it's-- the hope is not yet all lost, because there are going to be younger Jews coming out from those camps. And then there were-- there was a question of having a sanatorium in-- in Italy where some of the people who had TB had to be placed. And some-- in each place, there was a specific problems of the Jewish population, that it necessary was-- to necessary to organize-- supply an organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I was doing was organization work. I was not working as a physician dealing clinically with the-- with the people, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --overseeing and organizing the help that they needed. And it was like I went to-- to-- to Czechoslovakia. There I was-- went to visit the home for ageds there and were deployed abroad there-- organization, health organization-- medicare-- care. So-- and that's what I was doing. And-- and it was quite a lot. So for example, at the one time, it was also Jews that were going from-- from Africa to-- to Palestine. In-- and in-- in Marseilles, in-- in France, they had to have a-- a-- a place where they had to be examined, because some of them had trachoma in the eyes. Some of them had TB. They had to have a-- to-- to have a x-ray to keep track of them. And-- and-- and then you had to have too a-- you need to know the supply of all of the things that are needed for people that body sleep in, and-- and hygiene about it, and what, a lot of those--

speaker: interviewer
text: Very important work.

speaker: interviewee
text: --exactly-- important work--

speaker: interviewer
text: Very.

speaker: interviewee
text: --of an administrative nature.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long did you do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked a year-- '46 and '47. American Joint Distribution Committee wanted me to work for them so they can start. And then I wanted to see the United States. So they made with me an agreement that they will send me to the United States for a year, and I will work for them a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I went to the American Joint. And I went to Johns Hopkins. I was accepted to four universities here. I selected Johns Hopkins.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got my master's degree in public health, in-- in Baltimore, and went back to Paris. That was '47, '48 academic year. During that year, '48. I got interested in mental health. And I got-- I had the need to talk about myself of what happened to me, of all of the losses and all that. I went to a-- to a psychiatrist in-- in Baltimore on March 6. And I worked with her-- Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] about three months. It was March, April, May. In June, I went back to Paris and worked in Paris for a year with the American Joint Distribution Committee. That's when I went to Czechoslovakia, and to Belgium, and to Italy. You know that-- what we did in Marseille-- all that. And then I-- well, I returned to the United States on my own, without anybody sending me or anything. And-- and I did a doctorate at Johns Hopkins. In 1950, I got a doctorate degree at Johns Hopkins in Public-- in mental health. I worked in a psychiatric hospital near Washington, DC-- that Chestnut Lodge in Rockville, Maryland. I don't know whether you heard about it. I was there. There was a woman, Frieda from Reichmann, who introduced in Chestnut Lodge the principles of intensive psychotherapy. She was-- she wrote a book, and she was teaching principles of intensive psychotherapy. And I was invited to New York City to teach her course. I took over that course at the Institute for the Crippled at The White Institute-- White Institute. When I came to New York, I worked at the Institute for the Crippled and Disabled. That's why I mentioned this. And-- and I got my training in psychoanalysis. In psychoanalysis in Baltimore and in Washington. And I worked in this hospital till 1953. In 1953, I moved to-- to New York, because I was going to get married to this Meyer Mendelsonow in 1954. It was 2nd of-- January 2, '54 I got married to-- to him. And I became a professor at Columbia Psychoanalytical Association in Psychiatric Institute here at Columbia. And I'm doing it. I'm still on the staff of this-- the-- Columbia and White Institute. I'm still teaching. I still see a few patients. And that's it-- in short. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's the story. As a closing statement, now that the war is over more than 50 years, what would you say your message would be?

speaker: interviewee
text: To whom?

speaker: interviewer
text: To the world-- about your experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I can-- I cannot-- a message-- life-- life it-- what it brings-- so it's not so much what you make of it as where it-- where it leads you. And life, living, is a very important-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --I don't-- not so much how important what you do, as-- living your life in a way that you can be-- [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] --appreciative that you are alive. Whichever-- whatever it brings, you take it as it comes. That's-- that's what I can say. I don't think that I have any messages.

speaker: interviewer
text: You would like to add anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to add anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: To add? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] No. I mean, I-- I would-- I would say that-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --probably what I'm feeling is tired now. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So to philosophize right now, apparently I don't see any-- any energy for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You really have a compelling story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: The thing-- the things that I experienced inside, which I was not talking about or sharing about, that might definitely exhausted me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand. Thank you so much for sharing with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a very special story--

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you. I thank you. for being interested in listening to it. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Well, that's me in 1942 or '43. It's down in Tashkent. It's the Russian way of doing photographs. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] This is me. And that's down in Baltimore, '47 or '48, at the School of Public Health. Somebody took a picture of me actually at a year. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That's my father, Gedalye Szalita. I am standing near to him, but-- and I don't-- cannot tell you exactly when it was taken, but I imagine around-- before '39. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's a photograph of my husband, Zenobi Pemow-- Pemow about-- must have been seven or eight years old. I don't know when it was taken. But seven or eight years old, it means in '17 or '16-- '17, '16, '18. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's my husband Zenobi Pemow. It must have been taken in-- in 1936. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Fanny. She might have been 10 or 11. It must have been taken in '35?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is-- this is me again. It must be taken sometime in the '60s. [PAUSES FOR 25 SECONDS] This is a portrait of me done by a Swedish painter, [NON-ENGLISH]. And he painted it in 1957, in Paris. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] It's not a bad job. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, this is a photo of a group of Jewish students in the gymnasium, Polish gymnasium, in Volodymyr-Volynskyi-- Volodymyr-Volynskyi-- Volodymyr-Volynskyi. And I am there. I don't know whether you want it marked-- boys and girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which row? Sitting? Standing? Just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Standing-- standing-- standing near with the-- from the right side, fourth. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm the fourth from either side.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was gymnasium, the name of Nikolai Kopernik-- Copernicus. [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] And it must have been done before 1929. Well, this, this was given about [NON-ENGLISH]. And it's in the Ukrainian language. And it is in 1941. [PAUSES FOR 22 SECONDS] Well, this-- this was written in-- during the war by [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the head of the-- of-- of the health department of the city of [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS] That's me and my cousin, Nomi, with her husband, Chaim and her son, Haggai. And we're near-- near the races in Upstate New York. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] Saratoga-- it's in Saratoga, Upstate New York.
